reasoning:
The forecast depends on whether 2025 will surpass 2024 as the hottest year on record, according to NASA's GISTEMP dataset. Following the strong El Niño in 2023-2024, La Niña conditions developed into early 2025. Historically, post-El Niño years are cooler than the peak El Niño year. Consequently, major climate models and institutional predictions early in the year expected 2025 to be cooler than 2024. Dr. Gavin Schmidt (NASA GISS director) and other groups originally projected 2025 to likely be the third-warmest year on record. 

However, temperatures in early 2025 have overperformed expectations. January 2025 was the warmest January on record globally, beating out January 2024, and February 2025 was the third warmest. Overall, Q1 2025 is tracking "close behind" the record-setting pace of 2024. Due to this persistent heat in spite of typical La Niña cooling, Berkeley Earth updated their probabilistic forecast in April 2025 to a 38% chance that 2025 will be a new record. In contrast, NOAA NCEI's April 2025 assessment placed the probability at a much lower 4%. Polymarket traded at 27% on May 1st. 

Given the divergence between tracking models, the statistical base rate (a post-El Niño year has never cleanly beaten a strong El Niño year historically, though 2023 defied models entirely), and the fact that 2025 is slightly lagging 2024 through Q1, a probability of 26% is well-calibrated. It balances the surprising Q1 heat and Berkeley Earth's optimistic 38% against NOAA's pessimistic 4% and the historical difficulty of breaking records during La Niña transitions.
